title: Retrieval and spacing design mastery gate

items.prompt: A learner must explain a mechanism without notes ten days from now. Which first action supplies the strongest new evidence for designing the next study step?
items.stimulus: The learner reread the explanation twice today and reports that every paragraph feels clear.

options.text: Reread the same explanation a third time and record how easy it feels.
Close the source, reconstruct the mechanism, and compare the attempt with a criterion-based answer model.
Copy the displayed summary into a new document with cleaner formatting.
Wait ten days without specifying any performance to attempt.

answer.summary: Attempt retrieval before review and judge the generated performance against declared criteria.
answer.reasoning: The closed-source reconstruction tests the future operation directly enough to reveal omissions. Comparison with an answer model then supports a targeted recovery action. Rereading or copying may support study, but neither supplies the same unaided performance evidence; waiting without a task creates a delay but no retrieval opportunity.
commonErrors: Selecting rereading because subjective ease is mistaken for evidence of later reconstruction.
Selecting waiting because spacing is mistaken for elapsed time without a scheduled retrieval operation.
Selecting copying because visible production is mistaken for generation from memory.

items.prompt: Design a twelve-day plan for a target currently classified as fragile. State the target performance, at least three retrieval opportunities including one changed-context task, the feedback and recovery step after each attempt, and a decision rule for adjusting the next opportunity. Explain why your sequence is evidence about durable performance rather than familiarity.
items.stimulus: The required final performance is to compare two already taught models and justify which model fits a new bounded case. The learner can name both models but currently confuses their selection conditions.

answer.correct: A complete response defines unaided model selection and justification as the target; schedules an initial retrieval, a delayed discrimination attempt, and a later changed-context selection task before day twelve; compares each attempt with criteria; prescribes targeted restudy and renewed retrieval after errors; and uses observed delayed performance to shorten, extend, support, or vary the next attempt. Exact days may differ when the reasoning is coherent and no universal interval is claimed.
answer.summary: Align every scheduled opportunity with the target operation, feedback, recovery, variation, and an adaptive decision rule.
answer.reasoning: The baseline is fragile because selection conditions are confused, so the first attempt should expose that distinction with support available after the attempt. Later opportunities should recur across time and require selection in changed cases. A calendar alone is incomplete: the plan becomes interpretable when each observation changes the next action. Repeated successful performance after delay and variation is stronger evidence than ease during repeated exposure, while still remaining a provisional judgment.
acceptableResponses: Any schedule with at least three separated retrieval opportunities before the target date, including changed-context model selection, provided the response states performance criteria, feedback, recovery, and an evidence-linked adjustment rule.
A plan that uses initially blocked discrimination practice and later interleaving is acceptable if the learner's initial representation of both models is made explicit.
commonErrors: Naming dates without specifying what the learner will generate or how it will be judged.
Placing answer review before every retrieval attempt.
Calling one immediate correct answer mastery.
Mixing unfamiliar models before ensuring a usable initial representation of each.
Claiming that the chosen day gaps are universally optimal.

items.prompt: Interpret the attempt record and prescribe the next retrieval opportunity. State what the record supports, what it does not support, and how you would change support, delay, or task form.
items.stimulus: Attempt 1, immediately after study: six of six criteria met with two prompts. Attempt 2, three days later: four of six criteria met without prompts; both missing criteria concern when the model does not apply. Attempt 3, four days later: six of six criteria met without prompts on the original case. The final assessment is seven days away and will use a new case.

answer.correct: The record supports improved unaided performance on the original case but not yet robust boundary discrimination in a new case. The next opportunity should occur before the final assessment and use a changed case that makes the two missing applicability criteria decisive. Feedback should compare the selection justification with those criteria; an error should trigger targeted recovery on the boundary followed by another changed-case retrieval, while success permits a later cumulative check closer to the required performance date.
answer.summary: Adapt the next task to the observed boundary error and the upcoming changed-case demand.
answer.reasoning: Prompted immediate success cannot establish unaided durability. The later original-case success is useful evidence, but it does not erase the prior boundary error or demonstrate transfer to the final format. Therefore task form must change, not merely repeat the original case. The seven-day horizon constrains when the next opportunity should occur, but it does not determine one uniquely correct day.
acceptableResponses: A response may choose any defensible day before the final assessment if it uses a changed case, targets the missing applicability conditions, includes criterion-based feedback, and states a recovery or expansion rule.
commonErrors: Repeating only the original case because it was successful.
Ignoring the two boundary criteria that failed.
Prescribing a day without specifying the retrieval operation and response to error.

items.prompt: Redesign the peer session and its follow-up. Your response must preserve individual retrieval, use group comparison as feedback rather than answer copying, schedule a later changed-context attempt, and state how different performance patterns change each learner's next step.
items.stimulus: Four learners have forty minutes together. They each classified three topics at baseline, but their fragile topics differ. Their old routine was to reread one shared chapter aloud. They need to use the ideas independently in a seminar nine days later.

answer.correct: A strong redesign allocates a brief individual closed-source attempt on each learner's selected fragile target, then uses criterion-based peer comparison to expose missing relations and assign targeted recovery. The group next compares or selects among already introduced ideas only where that discrimination is part of the seminar demand. Each learner records a later individual changed-context retrieval before day nine. Missing core relations trigger supported recovery and an earlier renewed attempt; accurate but case-bound responses trigger a varied case; accurate delayed responses permit a longer gap and cumulative check. The response explains why aloud rereading alone does not supply the same individual performance evidence.
answer.summary: Adapt the retrieval loop to shared time without replacing individual generation with collective exposure.
answer.reasoning: The coordination constraint is new: one shared activity cannot target four different fragile baselines. Individual retrieval preserves diagnostic evidence, while structured comparison makes feedback efficient. Later individual retrieval is still required because successful group discussion can conceal who can reconstruct the idea independently and after delay.
acceptableResponses: Different allocations of the forty minutes are acceptable if every learner retrieves before feedback, receives target-specific recovery, schedules a later changed-context task, and has a performance-linked decision rule.
commonErrors: Using the most confident group member's explanation as a substitute for each learner's attempt.
Scheduling only the group meeting with no later retrieval.
Giving every learner the same next step despite different baseline and attempt evidence.
Calling discussion interleaving when no category or strategy selection is required.

criteria.description: Selects the closed-source retrieval attempt with criterion-based comparison.
Defines the required target operation and aligns at least three retrieval opportunities, including a changed context, with it.
Specifies criterion-based feedback, targeted recovery, and renewed retrieval after error.
Uses the baseline, retention horizon, delay, variation, and performance-linked adjustment rule without claiming one universal interval.

rubrics.title: Interpret and adapt an attempt record

criteria.description: Distinguishes what the sequence supports from the unsupported claim of general mastery.
Targets the observed boundary error with a changed-case retrieval and relevant criteria.
Links error and success patterns to specific recovery, support, variation, or spacing decisions.

rubrics.title: Transfer the loop to a study group

criteria.description: Preserves individual generation before group feedback and distinguishes retrieval from shared rereading.
Uses the group for criterion-based comparison and assigns recovery from learner-specific evidence.
Schedules a later changed-context attempt and adapts each next step to the observed performance pattern.
